did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach, specifically a custom 27-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), to classify melanoma skin cancer (MSC) from skin lesion images. The model extracts features such as edges, shapes, and patterns to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma classes, achieving an accuracy of 99.99% on publicly available datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors propose and evaluate a custom 27-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classifying melanoma skin cancer. It includes specific sections for 'Related Works' (brief literature context), 'Results' (experimental metrics like 99.99% accuracy), and 'Discussion' comparing their new model's performance against prior studies, rather than synthesizing existing literature as a review would.

title: Classification of melanoma skin cancer using deep learning approach
journal: TELKOMNIKA Telecommunication Computing Electronics and Control
brief_summary: This study proposes a custom 27-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify melanoma skin cancer (MSC) from skin lesion images. The model was trained and evaluated on a publicly available Kaggle dataset containing 1,800 balanced images of benign and malignant moles. Preprocessing steps included converting images to grayscale, resizing them to 20x20 pixels, and applying various filters to reduce noise. The evaluation metrics included precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy, with the model achieving a perfect score of 99.99% on the test set. The results indicate that the proposed CNN outperforms several state-of-the-art methods and previous works in the literature. The authors conclude that this deep learning approach has the potential to assist dermatologists in the early detection of melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the classification task. The dataset contains 'malignant moles' labeled as Class 1. The paper discusses subtypes (superficial spreading, nodular, lentigo, acral) in the introduction but the experiment classifies them broadly as melanoma vs non-melanoma.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The dataset contains 'benign moles' labeled as Class 0. The introduction mentions BCC, SCC, and sebaceous carcinoma as types of NMSC, but the experiment uses a binary 'benign vs malignant' framework from a Kaggle dataset.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer (accounting for ~75% of NMSC) and in the Related Works section regarding Rezvantalab et al.'s study. Not a target class in the authors' own experiment.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer (accounting for ~24% of NMSC). Not a target class in the authors' own experiment.
Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer (accounting for ~1% of NMSC). Not a target class in the authors' own experiment.

Mentioned in the Related Works section regarding Rezvantalab et al. and Pomponiu et al. studies. The authors' dataset uses the term 'benign moles' rather than 'nevi' explicitly for their classes, though they are clinically related.
Mentioned in the Related Works section regarding Esteva et al.'s study. Not a target class in the authors' own experiment.
Mentioned in the Related Works section regarding Esteva et al.'s study. Not a target class in the authors' own experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesion images into melanoma (Class 1) and non-melanoma (Class 0) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classifying melanoma skin cancer (MSC)' and 'classify them into melanoma and non-melanoma classes'. The model uses a softmax function to output class probabilities for two discrete classes (benign vs malignant). Evaluation metrics include precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from Kaggle containing balanced images of benign and malignant skin moles. The dataset consists of two folders corresponding to the two classes of moles.

use.training: 70% of the dataset (1,260 images) was used for training the custom 27-layer CNN model.
use.testing: 30% of the dataset (540 images) was used for testing the model's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

Kaggle Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign.notes: The model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-score for both classes, indicating it correctly classified all test samples. The overall accuracy is reported as 99.99% in the abstract and conclusion, while Table 1 lists 99.9% for specific metrics. The model outperformed previous works listed in Table 2 (e.g., Rezvantalab et al. 98.16%, Rehman et al. 98.32%, Charan et al. 88.6%, Molina-Molina et al. 97.35%, Jinnai et al. 91.5%, Pomponiu et al. 93.62%, Esteva et al. 96%, Pham et al. 89.0%).

architecture_details.normalization: Batch normalization layers applied after convolution layers
architecture_details.padding: Valid padding mentioned in general CNN description; specific padding for model not explicitly stated beyond 'valid' formula reference

training_details.loss_function: Not reported (Softmax output implies categorical cross-entropy for binary classification)

hyperparameters.stride: Not reported (general description mentions stride=1 as example)

classification_or_output_head.description: Softmax function converts K-dimensional vector of real values into probabilities summing to one for binary classification (benign vs malignant)

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB images to grayscale using the formula GRAY = 0.30R + 0.59G + 0.11B
Gaussian blur (Gaussian smoothing) to remove noise and detail
Image histogram equalization for contrast enhancement
conclusions: The proposed custom 27-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved an accuracy of 99.99% in classifying melanoma skin cancer (MSC) from skin lesion images, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods and previous works in the literature.
The deep learning approach has the potential to assist dermatologists in the early detection of melanoma, which can lead to more effective treatment and improved patient outcomes.
The study demonstrates the effectiveness of deep learning techniques for medical image analysis and highlights the importance of carefully designing and optimizing CNN models for high performance.
Ablation studies confirmed that each layer in the proposed model contributes to its overall performance.
The model utilizes batch normalization to normalize the output of convolution layers, which accelerates the learning process and helps prevent overfitting.
important_remarks: The model was trained and tested on a small, highly curated Kaggle dataset (1,800 images) with extreme class balance (900 benign vs. 900 malignant), which does not reflect the real-world prevalence of melanoma and may lead to overoptimistic performance estimates.

Images were resized to 20x20 pixels, a resolution that discards critical dermoscopic details (e.g., pigment network, streaks) necessary for clinical diagnosis, raising concerns about the model's ability to generalize to clinical-grade images.

The reported accuracy of 99.99% is suspiciously high for a medical imaging task and likely results from data leakage or a trivially easy split within the small Kaggle dataset, rather than genuine generalization capability.

The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets (e.g., ISIC), making it impossible to assess the model's robustness to variations in image acquisition devices, lighting, and patient demographics.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the metrics, preventing an assessment of the statistical significance or stability of the results.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods in Table 2 is methodologically flawed, as it compares the proposed model's accuracy against other studies' metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) without ensuring comparable datasets or preprocessing pipelines.

The preprocessing pipeline converts images to grayscale and applies Gaussian blur, which removes color information (a key diagnostic feature in melanoma detection) and smooths lesion boundaries, potentially degrading clinical utility.

The authors claim the model can be applied in clinical settings to assist dermatologists, but the lack of clinical validation, interpretability analysis, and comparison against human experts undermines this claim.
